title: Planet Merge
excerpt: Drag, drop, merge. Combine matching planets to discover the biggest body in the system.

blocks.heading: How to play
blocks.body:
Move your cursor across the top of the playfield to aim. Click (or tap, on mobile) to drop the waiting planet. When two of the same planet touch, they merge into the next size up.

The planet you're holding is shown by the crosshair at the top. The planet coming after it lives in the NEXT panel on the side. Drop the wrong one in the wrong spot and the stack creeps upward, and the container is open at the top: overfill it and planets get pushed over the rim. There are two ways to lose. If a planet falls out of the container, the run ends. And if the board gets so crowded that there is nowhere left to drop, that ends it too. When a stacked planet is in the way of your aim, the waiting planet dims, winces, and shows a red cross: you can't drop there, slide over to a free spot.

It's one endless run that ramps across seven levels as your score climbs. Level 1 drops the small bodies plus Venus, with both superpowers available. Level 2 adds Stars to the pool. Level 3 stops Venus dropping. Level 4 turns off the Eliminate power, and Level 5 turns off Choose. Level 6 takes the rainbow shield off your shakes, so shaking turns risky. Level 7, the last one, is the auto earthquake: the shakes start firing on their own. Every level-up pops a card at the top of the screen telling you exactly what changed. And the LEVEL panel above the playfield always shows where you are: tap it any time for a card listing what drops right now, which powers are on or off, and what the next level takes away.

blocks.heading: Chains and superpowers
blocks.body:
Every drop starts a fresh chain. The counter resets to zero the moment a new planet leaves your cursor. No slow accumulation across drops, no time window.

Get 3 merges from one drop's cascade and you unlock "Choose Planet". A pair of arrows appears near the top of the playfield so you can swap the planet you're holding for any droppable before letting it fall.

Get 5 merges from one drop's cascade and you unlock "Destroy". Every planet on the board lights up with a red crosshair. Click any one and every planet of that exact size vanishes off the board.

Unused powers carry over until you spend them. The chain counter doesn't. Drop a new planet and you're back to zero.

The later levels take these away one at a time. Eliminate switches off at Level 4, and Choose switches off at Level 5, so the final stretch is pure stacking skill.

blocks.heading: Shakes and the rainbow shield
blocks.body:
Merging also fills the SHAKES meter on the side. When it fills up, tap it to shake the board: every resting planet pops upward, and the lighter the load stacked on top of it, the higher it flies. It's a way to unstick a messy pile or nudge two planets together. Each tap spends 10% of the bar (the last sliver counts as a full tap, so the meter always drains to zero and starts building again). Tap again while it's still shaking and the next jolt hits a little harder.

While you're shaking, a rainbow arch guards the top of the playfield and the lose checks pause, so a shake can never end your run, at least at first. Level 6 takes the rainbow away, and from then on a careless shake can throw a planet over the wall and out, costing you the game. Level 7 goes all the way: the button locks and the shakes fire by themselves. Every drop can set off a random burst of 1 to 6 shakes, so the board never sits still for long.

blocks.heading: The droppables
blocks.body: The small bodies fall from the top, and in the early levels Venus drops in with them. Everything else is born from a merge. The drop pool shifts as you level up: Stars are held back until Level 2, and Venus stops dropping at Level 3.

cards.heading: 1. Stars
cards.body: Tiny twinklers. Drop the most often. Two stars merge into a Moon.

cards.heading: 2. Moon
cards.body: Crescent-shaped, with a physics collider that follows the actual silhouette, so it nestles into gaps differently than a round body. Two moons make a Pluto.

cards.heading: 3. Pluto
cards.body: The dwarf planet, small but round. Two Plutos make a Mercury.

cards.heading: 4. Mercury
cards.body: Cratered rock. Two Mercuries become a Mars.

cards.heading: 5. Mars
cards.body: The red wanderer. Two Mars planets fuse into a Venus.

blocks.heading: Forged by merging
blocks.body: Everything from here on only exists if you make it. Each step up doubles roughly in mass and gets harder to set up.

cards.heading: 6. Venus
cards.body: Earth's twin in size. Two Venuses merge into Earth. It also falls from the top during the first couple of levels, before it's held back.

cards.heading: 7. Earth
cards.body: Home, blue and bold. Two Earths fuse into Uranus.

cards.heading: 8. Uranus
cards.body: Pale ice giant. Two Uranuses become Neptune.

cards.heading: 9. Neptune
cards.body: Deep blue and stormy. Two Neptunes form Saturn.

cards.heading: 10. Saturn
cards.body: Ringed beauty. The collider includes the rings as separate pieces, so they tangle with whatever's nearby. Two Saturns become Jupiter.

cards.heading: 11. Jupiter
cards.body: King of the gas giants. Two Jupiters fuse into the final body.

blocks.heading: 12. Sun: two of them and they're gone
blocks.body:
The Sun is the maximum-level body and the only one that doesn't merge upward. When two Suns touch they both vanish in a flash and award a big score bonus.

Getting there means juggling Jupiter pairs. A board with even one Sun is already long-game territory.

blocks.heading: The vibe
blocks.body:
Quiet space, soft glow, planets with faces. The whole game runs in a small dark window, with no chrome and no menus competing for attention. Score sits in the corner, the next planet hides in the side panel, and the playfield does all the talking.

Physics is deliberately a little arcadey. Heavy planets get a real shove when something light hits them, so chain reactions feel weighty even when the dropping planet is a tiny star. Sleeping bodies wake the instant anything below them merges, so a tower in the middle of a chain reaction collapses naturally instead of leaving floating planets in mid-air.

No timer pressure. The difficulty ramps on its own as your score climbs, but there's nothing to grind and nothing to unlock. Sit with one run, see how far you can take it, restart whenever.

blocks.text: Drop with intent. Chain with patience. Save the Destroy power for the moment the board needs rescuing, not the moment you earn it.
